research_question: What is heterogeneity I² threshold for substantial in meta-analysis interpretation? Cite Higgins/Thompson 2003 + Cochrane Handbook current thresholds (low/moderate/substantial/considerable).

findings.claim: Higgins and Thompson (2003, BMJ) proposed tentative I² threshold classifications: ~25% = low heterogeneity, ~50% = moderate, ~75% = high heterogeneity. These were explicitly framed as tentative guidelines specific to Cochrane RCT meta-analyses, not absolute rules.

The Cochrane Handbook distinguishes four I² levels: low, moderate, substantial, and considerable heterogeneity. However, current Cochrane guidance explicitly warns against mechanical application of threshold cutpoints and recommends integrating I² with confidence intervals, prediction intervals, forest plot inspection, and investigation of heterogeneity sources.

I² estimates are unstable when a meta-analysis includes fewer than ~500 events or fewer than 15 trials; no major fluctuations are observed after 500 events and 14 trials. Cochrane recommends always reporting I² with its 95% confidence interval rather than the point estimate alone.

A fundamental limitation of I² threshold classification is that I² measures the proportion of variability attributable to heterogeneity, not the absolute magnitude of between-study effect variation. Two meta-analyses with identical I² = 50% may have RR ranges of 0.95–1.05 vs. 0.70–1.40, reflecting very different clinical situations. I² >85% also shows little further increase even as between-studies variance grows substantially.

The original Higgins/Thompson 2003 paper also introduced the H and R statistics alongside I². H = sqrt(Q/df); R = ratio of SE from random-effects to SE from fixed-effect meta-analysis. I² = 100% × (Q − df)/Q. The Q-statistic was the prior standard but suffers low power (few studies) and excessive power (many studies) compared to I².

convergence_flags.description: Higgins/Thompson 2003 thresholds (25/50/75%) consistently cited across Cochrane and methodological literature.
Contemporary Cochrane guidance consistently deemphasizes threshold-based interpretation in favor of prediction intervals and integrated assessment.

Exact numerical I² cutpoints for 'substantial' and 'considerable' in the current Cochrane Handbook edition not confirmed numerically from chapter text; Cochrane avoids publishing fixed cutpoints in recent editions.

gaps: Current Cochrane Handbook (6th ed.) deliberately avoids fixed numerical cutpoints for 'substantial' and 'considerable' I² categories; exact numbers not extractable from available text.
PMC3405079 PMID not confirmed (URL-referenced only; PMID 22516953 is a candidate).
